figures.caption: Figure 1: Overview of LLM-based multi-agent systems organized along an operational lifecycle. The right side summarizes representative topics and systems in each stage, and the left-side axis visualizes the cross-stage progression from action to evolution.
Table 11: Comparison of systemic self-evolution methods (Section 5.3.2).
Table 8: Comparison of multi-agent failure attribution methods (Section 4).

Figure 8: Overview of the multi-agent self-evolution framework. The top panel details the continuous self-evolution cycle driven by variation, selection, and retention, incorporating environmental rewards, peer-agent critiques, and self-evaluation as selection pressures. The bottom panel categorizes the evolutionary targets into three hierarchical levels: Agentic level, Systemic level, and Meta level.
Table 9: Comparison of multi-agent failure attribution datasets.

Figure 7: Scenario illustration of anomaly attribution evaluation in multi-agent systems. It presents three input case types—single-point, propagation-chain, and multi-cause failures—together with the corresponding multigranularity attribution targets and evaluation criteria.

Figure 2: Illustrative overview of the operational lifecycle for LLM-based multi-agent systems, using a collaborative travel-planning scenario as a running example. The winding pathway connects four causally linked stages from Start to Destination: ❶ Individual Intelligence, ❷ Multi-Agent Collaboration, ❸ Multi-Agent Attribution, and ❹ Multi-Agent System Self-Evolution. Each stage is accompanied by a taxonomy panel summarizing the key dimensions and sub-topics reviewed in this survey.
Table 3: Comparison of planning enhancement methods (Section 2.4), organized by planning paradigm.
Table 5: Single-agent capability benchmarks, organized by evaluation domain.

Figure 3: Architecture of individual agent capabilities organized along the reasoning pipeline. Memory serves as a cross-cutting module throughout this process, providing retrieved context and incorporating execution feedback. Planning and tool use are further elaborated as core components of the processing stage.
Figure 6: Mechanism of Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems.
Figure 5: Evaluation benchmarks for multi-agent collaboration. This figure compares existing benchmarks across task components, evaluation samples, and evaluation metrics.
Table 1: Comparison of reasoning enhancement methods (Section 2.2), organized by intervention stage and sub-type.

Figure 4: Overview of the multi-agent collaboration system. The right panel details a three-tiered design framework comprising the Foundation, Structure, and Execution layers to categorize agent roles, communication modes, orchestration topologies, and interaction dynamics. The left panel outlines the bottom-up system lifecycle, progressing from concept and domain modeling through to performance evaluation and feedback.
Table 7: Comparison of multi-agent frameworks, organized by collaboration design.
Table 6: Comparison of agent communication protocols.
Table 4: Comparison of tool-use enhancement methods (Section 2.5).
Table 2: Comparison of memory enhancement methods (Section 2.3), organized along the memory lifecycle.
